text: The surgeon retracts the left lobe of the liver, providing better exposure to the surgical field beneath.
The surgeon uses a Babcock clamp placed just below the cardia to pull the esophagus downwards and laterally, facilitating exposure for the cardiomytomy procedure.
The surgeon divides the gastrohepatic ligament, which is visible as a thin membranous structure connecting the stomach to the liver.
The surgeon divides the anterior frenoesophageal ligament, exposing the anterior surface of the esophagus.

Following the division of the phrenoesophageal ligament, the surgeon develops the plane between the right crus and the esophagus, entering the retroesophageal space and further expanding it using blunt dissection.
The surgeon exposes the anterior surface of the esophagus by dividing the anterior frenoesophageal ligament, revealing the esophagus beneath.
The surgeon exposes approximately seven centimeters of the esophagus above the gastroesophageal junction, preparing the area for the upcoming myotomy procedure.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection of the esophagus trans-hiatally, applying downward traction using a Babcock clamp to pull the esophagus down just below the cardia.
The surgeon dissects the pad of fat at the gastroesophageal junction away from the bowel wall, carefully exposing the cardia for two to three centimeters below the junction.
The surgeon mobilizes the gastroesophageal junction pad of fat, revealing the bare area of the cardia of the stomach with visible muscle fibers not covered by serosa.
The surgeon prepares to split the muscle fibers in the lower part of the myotomy, focusing on the exposed anterior surface of the esophagus above the GE junction.
The medial surface of the esophagus is fully exposed, revealing approximately seven centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon exposes the cardia of the stomach, extending from three centimeters below the gastroesophageal junction to seven centimeters above it, revealing the muscle fibers of the stomach.

The surgeon initiates the esophageal myotomy by splitting the esophageal muscle fibers. Two blunt instruments are used to apply lateral traction on either side of the esophagus, visibly separating the fibers.
The surgeon continues the myotomy until reaching the smooth, glistening esophageal mucosal surface, which visibly pouts out as the muscle fibers are split.
The surgeon continues the myotomy by splitting the esophageal fibers, encountering some oozing during the procedure, which requires careful and time-consuming manipulation.

The surgeon creates a plane between the esophageal muscle fibers and the mucosa using gentle blunt dissection. Once a clean plane is established, the esophageal muscles are transected with a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon performs a quick and complete esophageal myotomy using a technique that minimizes bleeding, visibly separating the esophageal muscle fibers from the mucosa.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the esophageal muscle fibers, ensuring the inactive blade is positioned on the side facing the glistening esophageal mucosa.

The surgeon continues the myotomy, splitting the esophageal muscle fibers from below upwards along the esophagus, extending approximately seven to eight centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon completes the esophageal myotomy up to seven centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction and begins the gastric myotomy, extending three centimeters below the junction.
The surgeon splits the gastric circular, longitudinal, and oblique muscle fibers using a harmonic scalpel, following the established technique for a bloodless myotomy.

The surgeon performs blunt stripping and stretching to tear the densely adherent oblique fibers of the stomach, avoiding mucosal injury. After completing the esophageal myotomy, a puncture test is conducted.
The surgeon fills the upper quadrant with saline and installs air into the esophagus using a Ryle's tube to visually check for any puncture in the esophageal wall.
The surgeon identifies a mucosal breach and closes it using a PDA stitch, ensuring the integrity of the mucosal layer.
Following the completion of the myotomy and puncture test, the surgeon prepares to perform a fundoplication to prevent reflux.
The preferred method of fundoplication is a partial fundoplication.
The surgeon performs an anterior 180-degree Dor fundoplication, wrapping the stomach partially around the esophagus to prevent reflux.
This is because patients with achalasia have weak or absent esophageal body peristalsis and doing a three sixty degree fundoplication would lead to severe dysphagia.

In addition to preventing reflux, the additional advantages offered by an anterior door fundoplication are that the fundoplication keeps the edges of the esophageal walls apart and also in case of any subclinical mucosal breach, the fundoplication gastric patch acts as a buttress.
The surgeon secures the gastric fundal patch by suturing it to both the medial cartilage of the esophagus and the medial crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon performs the anterior 180-degree fundoplication using non-absorbable Ethibond sutures, demonstrating the use of a slip knot to tighten the sutures effectively.
The completed Heller's cardiomyotomy is shown alongside the anterior 180-degree door fundoplication, highlighting the surgical outcome.
